FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from Year 3 (2019-2022) with 10,048 adolescents aged 12-13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables: screen time (hours/day), problematic social media use (SMAQ), video game use (VGAQ), mobile phone use (MPIQ)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with coefficients reported; e.g., parental screen time modeling: B=0.66 (95% CI 0.36, 0.96) for total screen time, p<0.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values present in Table 3: coefficients (B), 95% confidence intervals, and p-values for all associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical analysis: cross-sectional analysis with inferential statistics, multiple linear regression models

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Paper reports cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as dependent variable, but includes parental monitoring and limiting of screen time as predictors (behavioral interventions, not purely demographic/contextual), and reports associations with multiple outcomes including problematic screen use which are not purely demographic-driven relationships.
notes: Paper measures phone/SM variables both as outcome (screen time outcomes) and relates to parental behaviors. Mixed-modality approach with separate extraction of phone/social media/video game specific coefficients (E2: separate rows for different screen modalities). All descriptive statistics included plus inferential tests (E8).

FT_IC1.evidence: Review of ABCD Study data with over 11,000 participants; explicitly states it reviews findings from ABCD Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Discusses specific smartphone/social media variables: screen time, social media use, video games, video chat, texting

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available; abstract indicates review of associations but does not specify whether this is an original analysis with inferential statistics or a literature review without primary analysis

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract does not provide numeric coefficients or statistics

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF available; title indicates 'review of findings' suggesting it may be a review article rather than original empirical analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2025) with DOI 10.1097/mop.0000000000001462

FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine FT-EC7 applicability without PDF. If article is a review, it would not report original analyses and would be excluded on FT-IC3/FT-IC5 grounds before FT-EC7 evaluation.
notes: No PDF provided; cannot fully evaluate FT-IC3, FT-IC4, FT-IC5 without access to full text. Title suggests review article, which if confirmed would result in NOT_MET for FT-IC5 (review, not original empirical analysis). E10 applies if this is a review-only with no original analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from period 2 follow-up (2018-2020) to period 3 follow-up (2019-2021) with 9,095 adolescents aged 10-13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures cyberbullying (lifetime and past 12-month) as exposure variable. While cyberbullying involves online/social media context, the paper does not separately measure a specific smartphone or social media variable as defined in FT-IC2. Cyberbullying alone without separately measured phone/SM variable fails E6.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with inferential statistics: lifetime cyberbullying B=0.41 (95% CI 0.18-0.65, p=0.001); past 12-month cyberbullying B=0.38 (95% CI 0.11-0.66, p=0.007)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients, confidence intervals, and p-values reported in Table 2 and text

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical prospective cohort analysis with longitudinal design (period 2 to period 3 follow-up, 1 year apart)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper excluded on FT-IC2 grounds (cyberbullying only, no separately measured phone/SM variable). FT-EC7 not reached.
notes: Paper measures cyberbullying as primary exposure but does not meet FT-IC2 requirement for specific smartphone or social media variable measurement. E6 applies: cyberbullying is measured but paper does not separately measure phone/SM variables needed for inclusion.
